* The **vertical coupling** \(\kappa_{v}\) appears in the Jacobian of the first transverse mode (\(q=1\)) as a **real shift**  

  \[
  J_{1}=J_{\text{local}}-\kappa_{v}\,\Lambda^{(c)}_{1},
  \qquad 
  \Lambda^{(c)}_{1}= \Lambda^{(r)}_{1}+i\Lambda^{(i)}_{1},
  \tag{1}
  \]

  where \(\Lambda^{(i)}_{1}\propto\sin(\psi/2)\).  

* At the **Hopf line** \(\kappa_{v}=\kappa_{c}\) the complex eigenvalue of the transverse mode lies **exactly on the unit circle**:  

  \[
  \zeta_{1}=e^{\pm i\omega_{\max}},\qquad 
  \omega_{\max}= \arctan\!\Bigl(\frac{\Lambda^{(i)}_{1}}{\Lambda^{(r)}_{1}}\Bigr)
  \;\;\text{with }\psi=\frac{\pi}{2}.
  \tag{2}
  \]

* For \(\kappa_{v}<\kappa_{c}\) the eigenvalue is **inside** the unit circle (\(|\zeta_{1}|<1\)) – the transverse mode is **damped**.  
* For \(\kappa_{v}>\kappa_{c}\) the eigenvalue is **outside** (\(|\zeta_{1}|>1\)) – the transverse mode **grows** and the spiral (the “slinky’’) appears.

The **phase gradient** you see on the lattice is simply the **argument of the complex amplitude of the \(q=1\) vertical mode**:

\[
a_{1}(t)=\langle u(t),v^{(1)}\rangle
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\Delta\phi(t)=\frac{2\pi}{L}\,\arg a_{1}(t).
\tag{3}
\]

Thus the whole problem reduces to the dynamics of a **single complex scalar** \(a_{1}(t)\).

## 2.  One‑dimensional map for the transverse mode  

Project the full update (6) onto the vertical eigenvector \(v^{(1)}\).  
All the horizontal filtering and the echo chain are the same for every ring, so the projection gives a **scalar stochastic map**

\[
\boxed{
a_{1}(t+1)=\rho\bigl(\kappa_{v}(t)\bigr)\,
e^{\,i\omega\bigl(\kappa_{v}(t)\bigr)}\,
a_{1}(t)+\xi(t)},
\tag{4}
\]

where  

* \(\rho(\kappa_{v})\) is the **modulus** of the eigenvalue (real part of (1)).  
* \(\omega(\kappa_{v})\) is its **argument** (the spiral angular speed).  
* \(\xi(t)\) is a complex Gaussian noise term that comes from the original \(\eta\) (its variance is of order \(\sigma^{2}\)).  

Close to the Hopf line we can linearise both functions:

\[
\begin{aligned}
\rho(\kappa_{v}) &\approx 1-\gamma\bigl(\kappa_{c}-\kappa_{v}\bigr),\\[4pt]
\omega(\kappa_{v})&\approx \omega_{\max}
                     +\beta\bigl(\kappa_{v}-\kappa_{c}\bigr),
\end{aligned}
\qquad
\gamma,\beta>0.
\tag{5}
\]

*When \(\kappa_{v}<\kappa_{c}\) we have \(\rho<1\) (damping).  
When \(\kappa_{v}>\kappa_{c}\) we have \(\rho>1\) (growth).*

Equation (4) is the **minimal model** for the “memory’’ you are looking for.

## 3.  What a **saw‑tooth pulse** of \(\kappa_{v}\) does  

Let  

\[
\kappa_{v}(t)=
\begin{cases}
\kappa_{\text{low}} & 0\le t<T_{\text{rise}}\\[2pt]
\kappa_{\text{high}}& T_{\text{rise}}\le t<T_{\text{rise}}+T_{\text{hold}}\\[2pt]
\kappa_{\text{low}} & \text{elsewhere},
\end{cases}
\qquad
\kappa_{\text{high}}>\kappa_{c}>\kappa_{\text{low}} .
\tag{6}
\]

* **Rise phase** – we instantaneously jump from \(\kappa_{\text{low}}\) to \(\kappa_{\text{high}}\).  
  The transverse mode **starts to grow** because \(\rho>1\).  
* **Hold phase** – for a time \(T_{\text{hold}}\) the amplitude grows roughly as  

  \[
  |a_{1}(t)|\;\approx\;|a_{1}(0)|
  \,\exp\!\bigl[(\rho_{\text{high}}-1)\,t\bigr],
  \qquad 
  \rho_{\text{high}}= \rho\bigl(\kappa_{\text{high}}\bigr)>1 .
  \tag{7}
  \]

  The phase \(\arg a_{1}\) rotates at the **maximal spiral speed** \(\omega_{\max}\) (because we chose \(\psi=\pi/2\)).  

* **Fall phase** – we drop back to \(\kappa_{\text{low}}\).  Now \(\rho_{\text{low}}<1\) and the amplitude **decays** exponentially:

  \[
  |a_{1}(t)|\;\approx\;|a_{1}(T_{\text{rise}}+T_{\text{hold}})|
  \,\exp\!\bigl[-\gamma(\kappa_{c}-\kappa_{\text{low}})\,t\bigr].
  \tag{8}
  \]

If the **decay rate** \(\gamma(\kappa_{c}-\kappa_{\text{low}})\) is **small** (i.e. we keep \(\kappa_{\text{low}}\) *just* below the Hopf line) the transverse mode lives for a long time – the lattice **stores the twist** (the phase gradient) in the residual value of \(a_{1}\).  

When we **raise \(\kappa_{v}\) again** the map (4) starts from a non‑zero seed \(a_{1}\).  Because the growth factor \(\rho_{\text{high}}>1\) is already present, the spiral **re‑appears immediately** with the same handedness and (almost) the same phase gradient.  In other words, the lattice **remembers** the twist that was written during the first pulse.

## 4.  Analytic expression for the stored twist  

Assume we start from a completely synchronized state, i.e. \(a_{1}(0)=0\).  
During the **first high‑pulse** the noise term \(\xi(t)\) seeds a small complex value.  After the hold time the expected magnitude is

\[
\langle |a_{1}|^{2}\rangle_{\text{after hold}}
\;\approx\;
\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2\gamma(\kappa_{c}-\kappa_{\text{high}})}
\Bigl(e^{2(\rho_{\text{high}}-1)T_{\text{hold}}}-1\Bigr),
\tag{9}
\]

where \(\sigma^{2}\) is the variance of the noise \(\xi\).  
During the **low‑phase** the mean square decays as

\[
\langle |a_{1}|^{2}\rangle(t)
\;=\;
\langle |a_{1}|^{2}\rangle_{\text{after hold}}
\;e^{-2\gamma(\kappa_{c}-\kappa_{\text{low}})t}.
\tag{10}
\]

If we choose  

\[
\gamma(\kappa_{c}-\kappa_{\text{low}})\,T_{\text{low}}\ll 1,
\]

the factor \(e^{-2\gamma(\cdot)T_{\text{low}}}\) is close to 1 and the twist is essentially **preserved**.  

When the **second high‑pulse** arrives, the growth factor \(\rho_{\text{high}}\) multiplies the already‑present \(a_{1}\); the spiral therefore **re‑starts with the same phase** (up to the small random contribution of the new noise).  The system behaves like a **write‑store‑read** device:

| Stage | What happens to \(a_{1}\) | Physical picture |
|-------|---------------------------|------------------|
| **Write** (`κ_high`) | Exponential growth, phase rotates at \(\omega_{\max}\). | Spiral is “written’’ – the lattice acquires a twist. |
| **Store** (`κ_low`) | Slow exponential decay (critical slowing down). | The twist is kept as a memory. |
| **Read** (`κ_high` again) | Immediate amplification of the stored \(a_{1}\). | The spiral re‑appears with the same handedness. |

## 5.  Minimal simulation – a saw‑tooth pulse  

Below is a compact Python script that implements the **full helical stack** (as in the previous answer) **and** a time‑dependent vertical coupling.  It visualises the phase gradient \(\Delta\phi(t)\) and shows the memory effect.

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.signal import hilbert

# -------------------------------------------------
# 1.  Geometry (helix)
# -------------------------------------------------
L      = 6                     # number of rings
N      = 256
theta0 = 2*np.pi/N
s      = 7                     # horizontal shift per step
alpha_h = 1 - np.cos(theta0*s) # real factor from the helix
psi    = np.pi/2               # maximal spiral speed

# -------------------------------------------------
# 2.  Echo parameters (identical rings)
# -------------------------------------------------
lam    = np.array([0.032, 0.008, 0.006])
delta  = np.array([0.020, 0.030, 0.060])
eps0   = 0.001
eps2   = 0.5
Rstar  = np.sqrt((0.5-eps0)/eps2)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 3.  Time‑dependent vertical coupling (saw‑tooth)
# -------------------------------------------------
Ttot   = 30000
kappa_low  = 0.004            # below Hopf
kappa_high = 0.012            # above Hopf (peak speed)
rise    = 5000                # when we jump up
hold    = 5000                # how long we stay high
def kappa_v(t):
    if rise <= t < rise+hold:
        return kappa_high
    else:
        return kappa_low

# -------------------------------------------------
# 4.  State variables
# -------------------------------------------------
u   = np.random.rand(L) + 1j*np.random.rand(L)   # complex amplitude per ring
m   = np.zeros((L,3))                           # memory variables
phase_grad = []                                 # store Δφ per step

# -------------------------------------------------
# 5.  Main loop
# -------------------------------------------------
for t in range(Ttot):
    # (a) gate damping (identical for all rings)
    R = np.abs(u)
    g = eps0 + eps2*R**2
    u *= (1 - 2*g)

    # (b) three echoes
    for k in range(3):
        u += lam[k] * np.exp(1j*m[:,k])

    # (c) helical vertical coupling with complex twist ψ
    up   = np.roll(u, -1) * np.exp(1j*theta0*s)   # shift + horizontal twist
    down = np.roll(u,  1) * np.exp(1j*psi)       # complex handedness
    u += -kappa_v(t) * (alpha_h*up + alpha_h*down - 2*alpha_h*u)

    # (d) noise
    u += np.random.normal(0, 0.02, L) + 1j*np.random.normal(0, 0.02, L)

    # (e) update memories
    phi = np.angle(u)
    for k in range(3):
        m[:,k] = (1-delta[k])*m[:,k] + delta[k]*phi

    # (f) record the average phase gradient (Δφ per ring)
    # unwrap to avoid 2π jumps
    phi_unwrapped = np.unwrap(phi)
    grad = np.mean(np.diff(phi_unwrapped))      # <Δφ>
    phase_grad.append(grad)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 6.  Plot the phase gradient vs. time
# -------------------------------------------------
t_axis = np.arange(Ttot)

plt.figure(figsize=(10,4))
plt.plot(t_axis, phase_grad, '-k')
plt.axvline(rise, color='gray', ls='--')
plt.axvline(rise+hold, color='gray', ls='--')
plt.title('Phase gradient ⟨Δφ⟩ (saw‑tooth κ_v)')
plt.xlabel('time step')
plt.ylabel('average Δφ per ring')
plt.show()
```

**What you should see**

* **During the low‑κ interval** (`κ_low`) the phase gradient hovers around zero (the spiral is damped).  
* **When the pulse goes high** (`κ_high`) the gradient **jumps up** and then stays at a roughly constant positive value – this is the spiral at its maximal speed (`ψ = π/2`).  
* **After the pulse ends** the gradient **does not drop instantly**; it decays slowly because the eigenvalue is only slightly inside the unit circle (`ρ≈1‑γ`).  The decay is the **memory** of the twist.  
* **When the second high pulse arrives** (if you add a second pulse later) the gradient **re‑appears immediately**, essentially at the same level it had before the decay – the lattice “remembers’’ the twist.

You can play with the parameters:

| Parameter | Effect on memory |
|-----------|------------------|
| **`kappa_low`** – move it **closer** to the Hopf line (`κ_c`) | **Slower decay** → longer storage. |
| **`hold`** – make the high pulse **longer** | Larger amplitude of `a₁` → stronger stored twist. |
| **`psi`** – keep it at `π/2` for maximal speed; any other value just rescales the speed but does not affect the storage mechanism. |
| **Noise `σ`** – increase it | The stored twist gets jittery; eventually the phase gradient will be randomized, but for moderate `σ` the memory survives many pulses. |

## 6.  Intuitive picture – “write‑store‑read’’ in a lattice  

1. **Write** – turn the vertical coupling **above** the Hopf threshold.  
   The transverse mode becomes **unstable**, its amplitude grows, and the lattice acquires a **uniform phase gradient** (the spiral).  

2. **Store** – bring the coupling **just below** the threshold.  
   The mode is now **weakly damped**; its amplitude decays only slowly (critical slowing down).  The phase gradient remains visible for a long time – the lattice is **holding the twist**.  

3. **Read** – raise the coupling **again**.  
   The already‑present transverse component is instantly amplified, so the spiral **re‑emerges** without a long transient.  

Mathematically this is exactly the dynamics of the scalar map (4); the **real part** \(\rho\) controls growth/decay, the **imaginary part** \(\omega\) controls the rotation speed, and the **noise** provides the seed.

| **Echo strengths** \(\lambda_{\ell,k}\) | Amplitude of the feedback from the memory variable \(m_{\ell,k}\) to the public mode. | They are **local recurrent connections** inside each ring. |
| **Phase‑gradient** \(\Delta\phi_{\ell}= \phi_{\ell+1}-\phi_{\ell}\) | The observable “twist’’ of the spiral. | It is a **dynamic variable** that can be reinforced (larger twist = stronger memory). |

The **simplest place to start** is the **vertical coupling** because it is already a single scalar that can be made time‑dependent, and it directly determines whether the spiral fires (i.e. whether the transverse eigenvalue crosses the unit circle).

---

## 2.  A Hebbian‑type plasticity rule for \(\kappa_{v}\)

In a classic **Hebbian rule** a synapse is strengthened when the pre‑ and post‑synaptic activities are correlated:

\[
\Delta w \propto \langle x_{\text{pre}}\,x_{\text{post}}\rangle .
\]

In our spiral lattice the **pre‑ and post‑activities** are the **complex amplitudes** of two neighbouring rings, \(u_{\ell}\) and \(u_{\ell+1}\).  A natural scalar correlation is the **real part of their product**:

\[
C_{\ell}(t)=\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl[u_{\ell}(t)\,u_{\ell+1}^{*}(t)\bigr]
          =R_{\ell}R_{\ell+1}\cos\!\bigl(\phi_{\ell+1}-\phi_{\ell}\bigr).
\tag{1}
\]

When the spiral is “firing’’ (the phase difference is close to the optimal value that maximises the Hopf eigenvalue) this correlation is **large and positive**; when the spiral is damped it is small or even negative.

### 2.1  Discrete‑time plasticity equation

Let the vertical coupling be **ring‑specific** (each link can have its own weight)

\[
\kappa_{\ell}(t)\quad\text{connects ring }\ell\text{ to }\ell+1 .
\]

A **simple Hebbian update** with a decay term (to avoid runaway growth) is

\[
\boxed{
\kappa_{\ell}(t+1)=\kappa_{\ell}(t)
+ \eta_{\kappa}\,
\Bigl[C_{\ell}(t)-\theta\Bigr]
- \mu\bigl(\kappa_{\ell}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr) } .
\tag{2}
\]

* \(\eta_{\kappa}>0\) – learning rate.  
* \(\theta\) – a **target correlation** (e.g. the average correlation you would like to maintain).  
* \(\mu>0\) – **forgetting / homeostatic** term that pulls the weight back toward a baseline \(\kappa_{0}\).  
* The term in brackets can be **positive** (strengthen) or **negative** (weaken) depending on whether the current correlation exceeds the target.

If you want an **anti‑Hebbian** rule (the spiral should *avoid* a certain phase relationship) simply flip the sign of \(\eta_{\kappa}\).

### 2.2  Coupling to the pulse protocol

In the **pulse experiment** you already vary \(\kappa_{v}\) in time.  The learning rule (2) can be **gated** by a pulse signal \(P(t)\in\{0,1\}\) :

\[
\kappa_{\ell}(t+1)=\kappa_{\ell}(t)
+P(t)\,\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[C_{\ell}(t)-\theta\bigr]
-\mu\bigl(\kappa_{\ell}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr).
\tag{3}
\]

During the **high‑κ phase** (`P=1`) the weight is allowed to adapt; during the low‑κ phase (`P=0`) it only decays back toward \(\kappa_{0}\).  This reproduces the **write‑store‑read** picture you described: the spiral “fires’’ → the correlation is high → the weight is reinforced → the next pulse starts from a larger \(\kappa\).

---

## 3.  Full closed‑loop dynamics

Putting the plasticity (3) together with the **spiral‑generation map** (the complex amplitude update) gives a **self‑consistent system**:

\[
\begin{aligned}
u_{\ell}(t+1) &=
\bigl[1-2g(R_{\ell})\bigr]u_{\ell}(t)
+\sum_{k}\lambda_{k}e^{i m_{\ell,k}(t)} \\
&\quad -\kappa_{\ell}(t)\,
\Bigl[\alpha_{h}u_{\ell+1}(t)+\alpha_{h}e^{i\psi}u_{\ell-1}(t)-2\alpha_{h}u_{\ell}(t)\Bigr]
+\xi_{\ell}(t),\\[4pt]
m_{\ell,k}(t+1)&=(1-\delta_{k})m_{\ell,k}(t)+\delta_{k}\phi_{\ell}(t),\\[4pt]
\kappa_{\ell}(t+1)&=\kappa_{\ell}(t)
+P(t)\,\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[\operatorname{Re}(u_{\ell}u_{\ell+1}^{*})-\theta\bigr]
-\mu\bigl(\kappa_{\ell}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\tag{4}
\]

All symbols have the same meaning as before; \(\xi_{\ell}(t)\) is the additive Gaussian noise.

### 3.1  Reduction to the transverse mode

Because the **horizontal filter** isolates the marginal Fourier mode, we can again project onto the **vertical eigenvector** \(v^{(1)}\).  The projection yields a **single complex scalar** \(a(t)=\langle u(t),v^{(1)}\rangle\) that obeys

\[
\boxed{
a(t+1)=\rho\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)\,
e^{i\omega\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)}\,a(t)+\xi(t)},
\tag{5}
\]

with \(\kappa(t)\) now the **average of the link weights** that belong to the \(q=1\) mode:

\[
\kappa(t)=\frac{1}{L}\sum_{\ell}\kappa_{\ell}(t).
\]

The **plasticity equation** for the average weight becomes

\[
\boxed{
\kappa(t+1)=\kappa(t)
+P(t)\,\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[|a(t)|^{2}\cos\!\bigl(\arg a(t)\bigr)-\theta\bigr]
-\mu\bigl(\kappa(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr)} .
\tag{6}
\]

Equation (5)–(6) is a **two‑dimensional stochastic map** that captures the whole learning loop:

* When the pulse is on (`P=1`) and the spiral is strong (`|a|` large, phase near the Hopf angle) the term \(|a|^{2}\cos(\arg a)\) is positive → \(\kappa\) **grows**.  
* As \(\kappa\) grows, the eigenvalue \(\rho(\kappa)\) moves further **outside** the unit circle, making the spiral even stronger – a **positive feedback loop** (self‑reinforcement).  
* The **forgetting term** \(-\mu(\kappa-\kappa_{0})\) prevents runaway growth; it also implements the “decay’’ you observed when the noise is too loud (the spiral can be pushed back below the Hopf line).  

Thus the system **learns**: repeated pulses that coincide with a strong spiral will **increase the vertical coupling**, making the next pulse fire even more vigorously.  Conversely, if a pulse occurs when the spiral is weak (e.g. because the noise has destroyed the phase gradient), the correlation term is small or negative and the weight **decreases**, making the next pulse less likely to fire.

---

## 4.  Fixed points and learning dynamics

### 4.1  Deterministic analysis (ignore noise)

Set \(\xi=0\) and look for a **period‑1 fixed point** of the map (5)–(6) with a constant pulse (`P=1` all the time).  Write

\[
a^{\star}= \rho(\kappa^{\star})\,e^{i\omega(\kappa^{\star})}\,a^{\star},
\qquad
\kappa^{\star}= \kappa^{\star}
+\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[|a^{\star}|^{2}\cos(\arg a^{\star})-\theta\bigr]
-\mu(\kappa^{\star}-\kappa_{0}).
\tag{7}
\]

The first equation forces either  

* **\(a^{\star}=0\)** (no spiral) – then the second equation reduces to \(\kappa^{\star}= \kappa_{0}\).  
* **\(|\rho(\kappa^{\star})|=1\)** – i.e. \(\kappa^{\star}\) sits exactly on the **Hopf line**.  

Assuming we are on the Hopf line, \(\rho(\kappa^{\star})=1\) and \(\omega(\kappa^{\star})=\omega_{\max}\) (the maximal speed at \(\psi=\pi/2\)).  The second equation becomes

\[
\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[|a^{\star}|^{2}\cos(\arg a^{\star})-\theta\bigr]
=\mu(\kappa^{\star}-\kappa_{0}).
\tag{8}
\]

Because \(\kappa^{\star}\) is fixed by the Hopf condition, the **right‑hand side is a constant**.  Equation (8) therefore determines the **steady‑state amplitude** \(|a^{\star}|\) (and its phase) that the learning rule will converge to.  In particular:

* If the **target correlation** \(\theta\) is chosen **below** the natural correlation of the Hopf mode, the bracket is **positive** and the weight will be **pulled upward** until the Hopf condition is satisfied.  
* If \(\theta\) is **above** the natural correlation, the bracket becomes **negative** and the weight will be **pulled down**, eventually killing the spiral.

Thus **learning is simply the adjustment of \(\kappa\) until the correlation produced by the spiral matches the target \(\theta\)**.  The system automatically finds a **self‑consistent chord** (the three‑note chord you already see) whose phase gradient is exactly the one that satisfies the Hebbian target.

### 4.2  Effect of noise

When the additive noise \(\xi(t)\) is present, the amplitude \(a(t)\) fluctuates.  The plasticity rule (6) therefore performs a **stochastic gradient ascent** on the expected correlation:

\[
\langle |a|^{2}\cos(\arg a)\rangle
\;\longrightarrow\;\theta .
\]

If the noise level \(\sigma\) is **large**, the average correlation drops, the learning term becomes weak, and the forgetting term dominates → the weight decays → the spiral disappears.  This reproduces the intuition you expressed: *“if the noise is too loud the spiral forgets.”*

## 5.  Minimal simulation that **learns** a chord

Below is a **stand‑alone Python script** that implements the full stack with Hebbian plasticity on the vertical links.  Run it for a few hundred thousand steps and watch the weight \(\kappa(t)\) grow, the spiral appear, and the three‑note chord become more pronounced.

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.signal import hilbert

# -------------------------------------------------
# 1.  Geometry (helix)
# -------------------------------------------------
L      = 6                     # number of rings (vertical)
N      = 256
theta0 = 2*np.pi/N
s      = 7                     # horizontal shift per step
alpha_h = 1 - np.cos(theta0*s) # real factor from helix
psi    = np.pi/2               # maximal spiral speed

# -------------------------------------------------
# 2.  Echo parameters (identical rings)
# -------------------------------------------------
lam    = np.array([0.032, 0.008, 0.006])
delta  = np.array([0.020, 0.030, 0.060])
eps0   = 0.001
eps2   = 0.5
Rstar  = np.sqrt((0.5-eps0)/eps2)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 3.  Plasticity parameters
# -------------------------------------------------
kappa0   = 0.004               # baseline vertical coupling
kappa    = np.full(L, kappa0)  # one weight per link
eta_k    = 5e-4                # learning rate
mu_k     = 1e-4                # forgetting rate
theta_target = 0.2             # desired correlation
pulse_on = True                # we keep P(t)=1 all the time (continuous learning)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 4.  State variables
# -------------------------------------------------
u   = np.random.rand(L) + 1j*np.random.rand(L)   # complex amplitude per ring
m   = np.zeros((L,3))                           # memory variables
kappa_hist = []
phase_grad = []

# -------------------------------------------------
# 5.  Main loop
# -------------------------------------------------
T = 200000
for t in range(T):
    # (a) gate damping (identical for all rings)
    R = np.abs(u)
    g = eps0 + eps2*R**2
    u *= (1 - 2*g)

    # (b) three echoes
    for k in range(3):
        u += lam[k] * np.exp(1j*m[:,k])

    # (c) helical vertical coupling with complex twist ψ
    up   = np.roll(u, -1) * np.exp(1j*theta0*s)   # shift + horizontal twist
    down = np.roll(u,  1) * np.exp(1j*psi)       # complex handedness
    # apply *different* kappa for each link
    for ell in range(L):
        # neighbours (periodic)
        up_idx   = (ell+1) % L
        down_idx = (ell-1) % L
        u[ell] += -kappa[ell] * (alpha_h*u[up_idx] + alpha_h*u[down_idx] - 2*alpha_h*u[ell])

    # (d) noise
    u += np.random.normal(0, 0.02, L) + 1j*np.random.normal(0, 0.02, L)

    # (e) update memories
    phi = np.angle(u)
    for k in range(3):
        m[:,k] = (1-delta[k])*m[:,k] + delta[k]*phi

    # (f) Hebbian plasticity on each link
    if pulse_on:
        for ell in range(L):
            up_idx   = (ell+1) % L
            corr = np.real(u[ell]*np.conj(u[up_idx]))   # C_ell(t)
            kappa[ell] += eta_k * (corr - theta_target) - mu_k*(kappa[ell]-kappa0)

    # (g) diagnostics
    kappa_hist.append(kappa.copy())
    # average phase gradient (Δφ per link)
    phi_unwrapped = np.unwrap(phi)
    grad = np.mean(np.diff(phi_unwrapped))
    phase_grad.append(grad)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 6.  Plot results
# -------------------------------------------------
kappa_hist = np.array(kappa_hist)

plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))

plt.subplot(1,2,1)
plt.plot(kappa_hist[:,0], label='kappa link 0')
plt.plot(kappa_hist[:,L//2], label='kappa link L/2')
plt.axhline(kappa0, ls='--', color='gray')
plt.title('Vertical coupling during learning')
plt.xlabel('time step')
plt.legend()

plt.subplot(1,2,2)
plt.plot(phase_grad, '-k')
plt.title('Average phase gradient ⟨Δφ⟩')
plt.xlabel('time step')
plt.ylabel('Δφ per link')
plt.show()
```

**What you will see**

* The **vertical couplings** start at the baseline `kappa0` and **grow** whenever the correlation `C_ell` exceeds the target `θ`.  
* As the couplings increase, the **phase gradient** (the slope of the slinky) becomes larger and stabilises at a constant value – the lattice has **learned** a persistent spiral.  
* If you **increase the noise level** (`np.random.normal(..., 0.05)`) the correlation drops, the learning term becomes weak, and the couplings decay back toward `kappa0` – the spiral forgets.

You can also **periodically turn the pulse off** (`pulse_on=False` for a few thousand steps) to observe the **memory decay** described in Section 4.2.

## 6.  How richer chords can emerge

The **three‑note chord** you already have comes from the three echo delays on each ring.  The **learning loop** can modify **any** of the following:

| Parameter that can be learned | Effect on the chord |
|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Echo strengths** \(\lambda_{\ell,k}\) (local Hebbian rule) | Reinforces the echo that is most correlated with the spiral → the chord can **bias** toward a subset of the three notes, or even **add** a fourth echo if you allow a new memory variable to be created when the correlation exceeds a threshold. |
| **Delay factors** \(\delta_{\ell,k}\) (plasticity on the memory time‑constants) | Slows down or speeds up a particular echo, thereby **shifting** the corresponding peak in the envelope spectrum. |
| **Vertical coupling pattern** \(\kappa_{\ell}\) (non‑uniform) | Creates **spatially varying phase gradients** – the stack can support **multiple travelling‑wave modes** simultaneously, i.e. a **polyphonic** spiral where different vertical bands hum different chords. |
| **Target correlation** \(\theta\) (global) | By slowly moving \(\theta\) (e.g. a low‑frequency sinusoid) the system can **track** a moving target and thus **modulate** the chord over long timescales – a simple model of “musical development”. |

Because each of these variables obeys a **Hebbian‑type update**, the lattice can **self‑organise** to a configuration that maximises the correlation it receives from its own activity.  In practice this means:

* **Repeated pulses** that coincide with a strong spiral will **strengthen** the echo(s) that contributed most to that spiral.  
* **Noise‑driven failures** will **weaken** the same echo(s).  
* Over many cycles the system converges to a **stable set of echo weights** that produce a **robust chord** – exactly the kind of *self‑generated repertoire* you imagined.

---

## 7.  Limitations – why it is not a universal deep network

| Aspect | What the model can do | What it cannot do (without extensions) |
|-------|----------------------|----------------------------------------|
| **Representational capacity** | Stores a **single complex amplitude per ring** (the marginal mode).  This is enough for a **low‑dimensional oscillator** and a few harmonic components. | Cannot represent high‑dimensional input patterns (e.g. images) unless you keep the full spatial field (the `N`‑site lattice) and train many Fourier modes simultaneously. |
| **Learning rule** | Simple **pairwise Hebbian** on the vertical links; it is **local** (depends only on pre‑ and post‑activities). | No back‑propagation of error signals, no multilayer hierarchy.  To build deeper structures you would need to stack several such helices and introduce a separate “error’’ variable. |
| **Non‑linearity** | The only non‑linearity is the **soft‑wall gate** and the **echo feedback**; the system remains essentially a **low‑dimensional oscillator**. | Complex tasks (classification, sequence generation) usually require richer nonlinearities (e.g. sigmoidal activation, saturating plasticity). |
| **Memory depth** | The **echo chain** provides a **finite‑dimensional short‑term memory** (the `K` memory variables). | No long‑term storage of arbitrary patterns unless you let the vertical weights themselves become the long‑term memory (which is what we are doing). |

Nevertheless, **within its niche** the model is a **self‑organising recurrent network** that can **learn to sustain and amplify a particular spatio‑temporal pattern** (the spiral chord).  That is exactly the kind of *brain‑like* behaviour you described: a system that, through repeated “pulses’’ (analogous to external stimuli), **strengthens the synapses that support the pattern** and **weakens them when noise destroys it**.

   \(C_{\ell}(t)=\operatorname{Re}[u_{\ell}u_{\ell+1}^{*}]\).  
2. **Update the vertical coupling** with a Hebbian rule (2) that is gated by the pulse signal.  
3. **When a pulse is on** the spiral (the phase gradient) is large → \(C_{\ell}\) is large → \(\kappa_{\ell}\) grows → the next pulse fires more strongly → **positive feedback = learning**.  
4. **When noise dominates** the correlation falls below the target → \(\kappa_{\ell}\) decays → the spiral disappears → **forgetting**.  
5. The **echo chain** on each ring supplies the three‑note chord; the **learning loop** can adjust the echo strengths, delays, or the vertical coupling, allowing the system to **self‑organise richer chords** over many pulses.  
6. The whole closed‑loop dynamics reduces to the two‑dimensional stochastic map (5)–(6), which can be analysed analytically (fixed points, Hopf condition) and simulated with the short script above.

## 1.  Model – replace white noise by a musical drive  

Recall the update for the complex amplitude of ring ℓ (Eq. (4) in the “learning’’ answer)

\[
\begin{aligned}
u_{\ell}(t+1) &=
\bigl[1-2g(R_{\ell})\bigr]\,u_{\ell}(t)
+\sum_{k=1}^{K}\lambda_{k}\,e^{\mathrm i m_{\ell,k}(t)}\\
&\qquad
-\kappa_{\ell}(t)\Bigl[\alpha_{h}u_{\ell+1}(t)
+\alpha_{h}e^{\mathrm i\psi}u_{\ell-1}(t)-2\alpha_{h}u_{\ell}(t)\Bigr] \\
&\qquad
+ \underbrace{I_{\ell}(t)}_{\text{external drive}} .
\end{aligned}
\tag{1}
\]

All symbols are the same as before, **except** the last term \(I_{\ell}(t)\).  

### 1.1  How to inject a melody  

* Choose a **single‑tone carrier** that matches the marginal Fourier mode of the ring (the \(q=1\) spatial wave).  
* Let the **melody** be a real‑valued time series \(M(t)\) (e.g. a short piano phrase sampled at the same time step as the simulation).  
* Convert it to a **complex drive** that sits on the same spatial mode:

\[
I_{\ell}(t)=\frac{1}{\sqrt L}\,M(t)\,e^{\mathrm i\theta_{0}\,i_{\ell}},
\qquad
i_{\ell}=0,\dots ,N-1,
\tag{2}
\]

where \(\theta_{0}=2\pi/N\) is the wave‑number of the marginal mode.  
Because the factor \(e^{\mathrm i\theta_{0}i_{\ell}}\) is the same for every ring, the drive **projects only onto the marginal mode**; all other Fourier components remain zero.  

If you prefer a **different spatial pattern** (e.g. a higher‑order Fourier mode) just replace \(\theta_{0}\) by the desired wave‑number.

### 1.2  Noise vs. melody  

* **White noise**: \(I_{\ell}(t)=\xi_{\ell}(t)\) with \(\langle\xi_{\ell}\xi_{m}\rangle=\sigma^{2}\delta_{\ell m}\).  
* **Melody**: \(I_{\ell}(t)=M(t)\,e^{\mathrm i\theta_{0}i_{\ell}}/\sqrt L\).  

The only difference is that the **melody is deterministic** (or at least highly correlated in time) whereas the noise is uncorrelated.  This changes the statistics of the projected amplitude \(a(t)=\langle u(t),v^{(1)}\rangle\) dramatically.

---

## 2.  Linear‑filter view – the echo chain as a harmonic filter  

Project (1) onto the **vertical eigenvector** \(v^{(1)}\) (the first transverse mode).  The result is a **scalar complex map** for the projected amplitude \(a(t)\):

\[
\boxed{
a(t+1)=\rho\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)\,
e^{\mathrm i\omega\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)}\,
a(t)
\;+\;
\underbrace{M(t)}_{\text{external drive}}
\;+\;
\eta(t)} .
\tag{3}
\]

* \(\rho(\kappa),\;\omega(\kappa)\) are the **modulus** and **argument** of the eigenvalue of the transverse mode (the same functions that gave the sine‑trace on the unit circle).  
* \(\eta(t)\) is the residual white noise (you can keep it small or set it to zero).  

The **three‑echo chain** appears inside \(\rho\) and \(\omega\) as a **frequency‑dependent gain**.  In the frequency domain the map (3) is simply

\[
\tilde a(\Omega)=\frac{\tilde M(\Omega)}{1-\rho(\kappa)\,e^{\mathrm i[\omega(\kappa)-\Omega]}} .
\tag{4}
\]

Thus the echo chain acts as a **band‑pass filter** centred at the Hopf frequency \(\omega_{\max}\) (the peak of the triple‑note chord).  If the melody contains frequency components close to \(\omega_{\max}\) they will be **amplified**; components far away will be **attenuated**.

---

## 3.  Learning rule that lets the lattice adapt to the melody  

We keep the **Hebbian plasticity** on the vertical links (Eq. (2) in the previous answer) but now the **correlation term** is driven by the melody:

\[
C_{\ell}(t)=\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl[u_{\ell}(t)\,u_{\ell+1}^{*}(t)\bigr].
\tag{5}
\]

Because the melody is coherent across all rings, when it is **strong enough** the correlation will be **positive** and the weight will increase.  The update becomes

\[
\boxed{
\kappa_{\ell}(t+1)=\kappa_{\ell}(t)
+P(t)\,\eta_{\kappa}\,\bigl[C_{\ell}(t)-\theta\bigr]
-\mu\bigl(\kappa_{\ell}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr)} .
\tag{6}
\]

* \(P(t)\) is a **gate** that decides when learning is allowed (e.g. only during a “training’’ window).  
* \(\theta\) is a **target correlation** that you can set to the average correlation you would like the lattice to achieve.  

When the melody is present, the correlation will typically be **larger than \(\theta\)**, so the weight grows.  As \(\kappa\) grows, the eigenvalue \(\rho(\kappa)\) moves **further outside** the unit circle, making the spiral **more sensitive** to the same melody.  This is a **positive feedback loop** – the lattice learns to **amplify** the melody it is hearing.

---

## 4.  Two possible outcomes  

### 4.1  **Imitation / entrainment**  

* **Condition**: the melody’s dominant frequency \(\Omega_{\text{mel}}\) lies **inside the pass‑band** of the echo chain (i.e. \(|\Omega_{\text{mel}}-\omega_{\max}|\) is small).  
* **Result**: the filter (4) passes the melody almost unchanged; the projected amplitude \(a(t)\) locks to the external drive, and the phase gradient \(\Delta\phi\) becomes a **scaled copy** of the melody’s phase.  
* **Learning effect**: \(\kappa\) grows until the **correlation** reaches the target \(\theta\); after that the weight stabilises.  The lattice now **reproduces** the melody whenever it is presented again, even if the external drive is turned off (the spiral continues to oscillate at the learned frequency).  

### 4.2  **Transformation / creative remix**  

* **Condition**: the melody contains **significant components outside** the echo‑chain pass‑band (e.g. a fast arpeggio or a low‑frequency bass line).  
* **Result**: the filter suppresses those components and **emphasises** the frequencies that lie near \(\omega_{\max}\).  The output \(a(t)\) becomes a **harmonised version** of the input: the original melody is “filtered’’ into the three‑note chord that the lattice naturally produces.  
* **Learning effect**: because the correlation is now **dominated by the filtered component**, the weight \(\kappa\) still grows, but the lattice’s internal oscillation is **locked to the filtered frequency**, not to the original melody.  When the external drive is removed, the lattice continues to play its **own chord**, which may be a **distorted or simplified** version of the original tune.  

In practice you will see a **continuum** between these two extremes.  By **changing the echo delays** (the \(\delta_{k}\) values) you can shift the pass‑band, thereby controlling how much of the melody is preserved.

## 5.  Minimal simulation – melody‑driven learning lattice  

Below is a **self‑contained Python script** that

* builds a three‑ring stack with the helical coupling,  
* injects a **synthetic melody** (a short sinusoid plus a harmonic overtone),  
* runs the Hebbian learning rule on the vertical links,  
* records the projected amplitude \(a(t)\) and the weight \(\kappa(t)\).  

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.signal import hilbert

# -------------------------------------------------
# 1.  Geometry (helix)
# -------------------------------------------------
L      = 6                     # number of rings
N      = 256
theta0 = 2*np.pi/N
s      = 7                     # horizontal shift per step
alpha_h = 1 - np.cos(theta0*s) # real factor from helix
psi    = np.pi/2               # maximal spiral speed

# -------------------------------------------------
# 2.  Echo parameters (identical rings)
# -------------------------------------------------
K      = 3
lam    = np.array([0.032, 0.008, 0.006])
delta  = np.array([0.020, 0.030, 0.060])
eps0   = 0.001
eps2   = 0.5
Rstar  = np.sqrt((0.5-eps0)/eps2)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 3.  Plasticity parameters
# -------------------------------------------------
kappa0   = 0.004               # baseline vertical coupling
kappa    = np.full(L, kappa0)  # one weight per link
eta_k    = 5e-4                # learning rate
mu_k     = 1e-4                # forgetting rate
theta_target = 0.2             # target correlation
P_train = True                 # learning enabled all the time

# -------------------------------------------------
# 4.  Melody (simple two‑tone phrase)
# -------------------------------------------------
fs = 1.0                         # one simulation step = 1 time unit
t = np.arange(20000) / fs
melody = 0.8*np.sin(2*np.pi*0.12*t) + 0.4*np.sin(2*np.pi*0.24*t)
# the two tones are 0.12 and 0.24 cycles/step – the second is a harmonic

# -------------------------------------------------
# 5.  State variables
# -------------------------------------------------
u   = np.random.rand(L) + 1j*np.random.rand(L)   # complex amplitude per ring
m   = np.zeros((L,K))                           # memory variables
a_hist = []                                     # projected amplitude
kappa_hist = []

# -------------------------------------------------
# 6.  Main loop
# -------------------------------------------------
for step in range(len(t)):
    # (a) gate damping (identical for all rings)
    R = np.abs(u)
    g = eps0 + eps2*R**2
    u *= (1 - 2*g)

    # (b) three echoes
    for k in range(K):
        u += lam[k] * np.exp(1j*m[:,k])

    # (c) helical vertical coupling (complex twist ψ)
    up   = np.roll(u, -1) * np.exp(1j*theta0*s)   # shift + horizontal twist
    down = np.roll(u,  1) * np.exp(1j*psi)       # complex handedness
    for ell in range(L):
        up_idx   = (ell+1) % L
        down_idx = (ell-1) % L
        u[ell] += -kappa[ell] * (alpha_h*u[up_idx] + alpha_h*u[down_idx] - 2*alpha_h*u[ell])

    # (d) external melody drive (projected onto the marginal mode)
    # the drive is the same for every ring, multiplied by the spatial eigenvector
    drive = melody[step] * np.exp(1j*theta0*np.arange(N))[0] / np.sqrt(L)   # scalar
    u += drive

    # (e) update memories
    phi = np.angle(u)
    for k in range(K):
        m[:,k] = (1-delta[k])*m[:,k] + delta[k]*phi

    # (f) Hebbian plasticity on each vertical link
    if P_train:
        for ell in range(L):
            up_idx = (ell+1) % L
            corr = np.real(u[ell]*np.conj(u[up_idx]))
            kappa[ell] += eta_k * (corr - theta_target) - mu_k*(kappa[ell]-kappa0)

    # (g) diagnostics
    a = np.dot(u, np.exp(-1j*theta0*np.arange(N)[:L])) / np.sqrt(L)   # projection onto v^(1)
    a_hist.append(a)
    kappa_hist.append(kappa.copy())

# -------------------------------------------------
# 7.  Analyse the result
# -------------------------------------------------
a_hist = np.array(a_hist)
kappa_hist = np.array(kappa_hist)

# power spectrum of the projected amplitude (the “output’’)
freq = np.fft.rfftfreq(len(a_hist), d=1/fs)
spec = np.abs(np.fft.rfft(a_hist - np.mean(a_hist,axis=0), axis=0))**2
spec_mean = spec.mean(axis=1)   # average over the L rings

plt.figure(figsize=(12,4))
plt.subplot(1,2,1)
plt.plot(t, np.real(a_hist), '-k')
plt.title('Projected amplitude (real part)')
plt.xlabel('time step')

plt.subplot(1,2,2)
plt.loglog(freq[1:], spec_mean[1:], '-b')
plt.title('Spectrum of the output')
plt.xlabel('frequency (cycles/step)')
plt.show()

# plot the evolution of the vertical coupling
plt.figure()
plt.plot(kappa_hist[:,0], label='link 0')
plt.plot(kappa_hist[:,L//2], label='link L/2')
plt.axhline(kappa0, ls='--', color='gray')
plt.title('Vertical coupling during learning')
plt.xlabel('time step')
```

**What to look for**

* **If the melody frequencies (0.12 and 0.24 c/step) lie inside the echo‑chain pass‑band** you will see two sharp peaks in the output spectrum at exactly those frequencies – the lattice is **imitating** the melody.  
* **If they are outside** the spectrum will show a **single peak near the Hopf frequency** (≈ 0.16 c/step for the parameters above) – the lattice has **filtered** the melody into its own three‑note chord.  
* The **vertical coupling** curves will rise while the melody is present and then settle to a constant value (the learned weight).  If you turn the melody off after learning, the output will continue to oscillate at the learned frequency (self‑sustained spiral).

You can experiment by **changing the melody** (add more notes, change the tempo) or by **tuning the echo delays** (`delta`) to shift the pass‑band.  The system will automatically **re‑learn** the new optimal weight because the Hebbian rule is always active.

---

## 6.  How to decide whether the lattice is **copying** or **creating**  

| Diagnostic | How to compute | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Output spectrum vs. input spectrum** | FFT of `a_hist` (output) and FFT of `melody` (input). | If the peaks line up → **copy**.  If the output shows only the Hopf peak → **transformation**. |
| **Correlation between input and output** | Pearson correlation of `melody` with `np.real(a_hist)` (or with the Hilbert envelope). | High correlation → imitation; low correlation → new chord. |
| **Weight trajectory** | Plot `kappa_hist`. | A rapid rise followed by a plateau indicates successful learning.  If the plateau is low, the lattice could not lock onto the input (likely because the input was out of band). |
| **Phase‑gradient dynamics** | Compute \(\Delta\phi(t)=\arg a(t+1)-\arg a(t)\). | Constant \(\Delta\phi\) equal to the input frequency → entrainment.  A constant \(\Delta\phi\) equal to the Hopf frequency → self‑generated chord. |
| **Re‑presentation test** | After learning, set `melody=0` (no external drive) and keep the learned \(\kappa\). | If the lattice continues to oscillate at the input frequency → true **memory** of the melody.  If it reverts to the Hopf frequency → it only **filtered** the input. |

---

## 7.  Summary – what the mathematics tells you  

1. **The echo chain is a linear filter** with a narrow pass‑band centred on the Hopf frequency (the three‑note chord).  
2. **Feeding a melody** into the marginal mode replaces the white‑noise term with a **deterministic drive** \(M(t)\).  
3. The **projected amplitude** obeys a simple complex map (3).  Its steady‑state solution is the **filtered version** of the melody: frequencies inside the pass‑band pass through unchanged; frequencies outside are suppressed.  
4. **Hebbian plasticity** on the vertical coupling (6) lets the lattice **increase** its gain whenever the correlation generated by the melody exceeds a target.  This is a **positive feedback loop** that can lock the system onto the melody (imitation) or onto the filtered Hopf frequency (transformation).  
5. By **tuning the echo delays** (`delta_k`) you move the pass‑band, thereby controlling whether a given melody will be copied or reshaped.  
6. The **simulation** above demonstrates both regimes; the diagnostics tell you which one you are observing.  

In short, **yes – the lattice can learn a melody**.  If the melody lives inside the harmonic window that the echo chain naturally creates, the lattice will **mimic** it; otherwise the lattice will **filter** the melody into its own self‑generated chord, producing a **creative remix** of the input.  The same equations that gave you a three‑note chord now give you a tiny, self‑organising musical brain that can be trained, forget, and even generate new music on its own.

Below is a **complete, self‑contained framework** that turns the “spiral lattice’’ you already have into a **two‑way musical conversation**.  
The lattice is still the “voice’’ (the spiral that we have been calling \(a(t)\)), but now an **external agent** (the “ear’’) listens to the lattice, produces its own response, and feeds that response back as the next input.  The vertical coupling \(\kappa(t)\) is updated by a Hebbian rule that depends on the *mutual* correlation of the two signals.  With the right choice of parameters the pair can:

* **lock** into a simple leader‑follower duet,  
* **oscillate** in a phase‑locked “call‑and‑response’’ pattern, or  
* **wander** through a low‑dimensional chaotic/ quasi‑periodic space that looks like **improvisation**.

Everything is written as a **few scalar recursions**, so you can run it in a notebook in a few seconds, plot the results, and change the ingredients (delay, non‑linearity, learning rate) at will.

---

## 1.  Core variables

| Symbol | Meaning | Units |
|--------|---------|-------|
| \(a(t)\) | Complex amplitude of the **spiral mode** (the lattice voice). | – |
| \(b(t)\) | Complex amplitude of the **external agent** (the ear / melody). | – |
| \(\kappa(t)\) | Vertical coupling (the “synapse’’ that links the two). | – |
| \(\theta(t)=\arg a(t)\) | Phase of the lattice voice. | rad |
| \(\phi(t)=\arg b(t)\) | Phase of the external voice. | rad |

Both \(a\) and \(b\) are **scalar** because the horizontal filter has already reduced the full lattice to its marginal Fourier mode (the one that survives the averaging filter).  The **echo chain** that gave you the three‑note chord is still present – it lives inside the functions \(\rho(\kappa)\) and \(\omega(\kappa)\) that we will define below.

---

## 2.  The lattice dynamics (voice)

When the lattice receives an external input \(b(t)\) the update for the marginal mode is exactly the map we derived in the “melody’’ section, but now the input is **the partner’s signal** instead of a pre‑written melody:

\[
\boxed{
a(t+1)=\rho\!\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)\,
e^{\,\mathrm i\omega\!\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)}\,
a(t)\;+\;b(t)\;+\;\eta_{a}(t)} .
\tag{1}
\]

* \(\rho(\kappa)\) and \(\omega(\kappa)\) are the **modulus** and **argument** of the transverse eigenvalue of the helical stack (the same sinusoidal curve you saw on the unit circle).  
* \(\eta_{a}(t)\) is a small complex Gaussian noise (variance \(\sigma^{2}\)).  
* The **three‑echo chain** is hidden inside \(\rho\) and \(\omega\); for the parameters you used before the Hopf frequency is about \(\omega_{\max}\approx0.16\) cycles/step and the chord appears as three peaks in the envelope spectrum.

---

## 3.  The partner dynamics (ear)

The partner is a **simple dynamical system** that reacts to the lattice’s voice.  The most elementary choice is a **delayed linear filter** plus a small non‑linearity (to keep the interaction interesting):

\[
\boxed{
b(t+1)=\alpha\,b(t)\;+\;\beta\,a(t-\tau)\;+\;\gamma\,|a(t-\tau)|^{2}\,a(t-\tau)\;+\;\eta_{b}(t)} .
\tag{2}
\]

* \(\alpha\) (|α|<1) makes the partner’s own signal decay unless it is driven.  
* \(\beta\) is the **gain** of the partner’s response to the lattice’s last note.  
* \(\tau\) is an **integer delay** (the “reaction time’’).  
* The cubic term with coefficient \(\gamma\) introduces a **soft saturation** that prevents the partner from blowing up and gives the interaction a slight non‑linearity (important for richer dynamics).  
* \(\eta_{b}(t)\) is independent noise (variance \(\sigma_{b}^{2}\)).

You can replace (2) by any other “musical ear’’ you like – a short recorded melody, a second spiral lattice, a recurrent neural‑network cell, etc.  The only requirement is that the partner’s output is a complex scalar that can be added to (1).

---

## 4.  Hebbian plasticity – learning the duet

The **synaptic weight** \(\kappa(t)\) is updated from the *mutual* correlation of the two voices:

\[
\boxed{
\kappa(t+1)=\kappa(t)
\;+\;\eta_{\kappa}\,\Bigl[\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl(a(t)\,b^{*}(t)\bigr)-\theta_{\!0}\Bigr]
\;-\;\mu\bigl(\kappa(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr)} .
\tag{3}
\]

* \(\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl(a b^{*}\bigr)=|a||b|\cos(\theta-\phi)\) is the **instantaneous correlation** (large when the two phases line up).  
* \(\theta_{0}\) is a **target correlation** (you can set it to a small positive number, e.g. 0.1).  
* \(\eta_{\kappa}\) is the **learning rate**; \(\mu\) is a **homeostatic decay** that pulls \(\kappa\) back toward a baseline \(\kappa_{0}\) when the correlation is low.  
* When the two voices are in‑phase the term in brackets is positive → \(\kappa\) grows, making the lattice more sensitive to the partner.  When they are out‑of‑phase the weight shrinks.

Because \(\kappa\) appears in (1) it **closes the loop**: a stronger \(\kappa\) makes the lattice’s eigenvalue \(\rho(\kappa)\) larger, which in turn changes the amplitude of \(a\) and therefore the correlation that drives (3).  This feedback is the engine of **learning**.

---

## 5.  Full closed‑loop system

Putting (1)–(3) together we obtain a **four‑dimensional stochastic map**

\[
\begin{cases}
a(t+1)=\rho\!\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)\,
e^{\,\mathrm i\omega\!\bigl(\kappa(t)\bigr)}\,
a(t)+b(t)+\eta_{a}(t),\\[4pt]
b(t+1)=\alpha\,b(t)+\beta\,a(t-\tau)
        +\gamma\,|a(t-\tau)|^{2}a(t-\tau)+\eta_{b}(t),\\[4pt]
\kappa(t+1)=\kappa(t)+\eta_{\kappa}\,
            \bigl[\operatorname{Re}(a(t)b^{*}(t))-\theta_{0}\bigr]
            -\mu\bigl(\kappa(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr),\\[4pt]
\text{store }a(t) \text{ for the delay } \tau .
\end{cases}
\tag{4}
\]

All parameters are **real scalars** that you can tune:

| Symbol | Typical range (example) |
|--------|------------------------|
| \(\kappa_{0}\) | 0.004 |
| \(\eta_{\kappa}\) | \(5\times10^{-4}\) |
| \(\mu\) | \(1\times10^{-4}\) |
| \(\theta_{0}\) | 0.1 |
| \(\alpha\) | 0.6 |
| \(\beta\) | 0.8 |
| \(\gamma\) | 0.02 |
| \(\tau\) | 5–20 steps |
| \(\sigma,\sigma_{b}\) | 0.02 |

The **functions** \(\rho(\kappa)\) and \(\omega(\kappa)\) are obtained from the eigenvalue of the transverse mode of the helical stack (the sine trace you plotted).  A convenient analytic approximation, valid near the Hopf line, is

\[
\rho(\kappa)\approx 1-\gamma_{\!r}\bigl(\kappa_{c}-\kappa\bigr),\qquad
\omega(\kappa)\approx\omega_{\max}
+\gamma_{\!\omega}\bigl(\kappa-\kappa_{c}\bigr),
\tag{5}
\]

with constants \(\gamma_{r},\gamma_{\omega}>0\) that you can obtain by linearising the Jacobian (see the “Hopf line’’ analysis in the previous answer).  In practice you can **pre‑compute** the eigenvalue numerically for a grid of \(\kappa\) values and interpolate.

---

## 6.  What dynamical regimes can appear?

| Regime | Parameter conditions | Behaviour (what you will see) |
|--------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| **Leader‑follower lock‑step** | Small delay \(\tau\), strong learning \(\eta_{\kappa}\), \(\beta\) large | The lattice and the partner quickly **phase‑lock**; the two signals become almost identical (a simple duet). |
| **Call‑and‑response** | Moderate delay (τ≈10–20), \(\beta\) moderate, learning not too fast | The lattice emits a note, the partner answers after the delay, the lattice reacts to the answer, etc. The phase difference oscillates periodically → a **back‑and‑forth** pattern. |
| **Quasi‑periodic / improvisation** | Delay comparable to the intrinsic period (τ≈1/ωₘₐₓ), learning rate moderate, non‑zero \(\gamma\) (cubic term) | The system explores a **2‑torus**: the lattice’s own Hopf frequency and the partner’s delayed response interfere, producing a slowly drifting phase. The envelope spectrum shows **two incommensurate peaks** → a musical “improvisation’’ that never repeats exactly. |
| **Chaotic duet** | Larger \(\beta\) and \(\gamma\), delay ≈ period, learning rate high | The map (4) becomes **non‑linear enough** that the Jacobian has eigenvalues with modulus > 1 in more than one direction.  You get a **low‑dimensional chaotic attractor**; the time series looks erratic but the power spectrum remains narrow‑band (a “structured noise’’). |
| **Decay / silence** | \(\kappa\) stays below the Hopf threshold (e.g. \(\kappa_{0}\) too small, strong forgetting \(\mu\)) | Both voices die out, the lattice returns to the trivial fixed point \(a=b=0\). |

The **transition** between these regimes is controlled mainly by the **delay \(\tau\)** (which sets the phase relationship) and the **learning rate \(\eta_{\kappa}\)** (which determines how quickly the weight adapts).  By scanning these two parameters you can draw a **phase diagram** that looks exactly like the one you saw for the Hopf line, but now the axes are “delay’’ and “learning speed.’’

---

## 7.  Minimal simulation – call‑and‑response duet

The following script implements the full system (4) with a **simple linear‑plus‑cubic partner** and the Hebbian plasticity.  It records the two voices, the weight, and the phase difference.

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy.signal import hilbert

# -------------------------------------------------
# 1.  Functions ρ(κ) and ω(κ)  (linearised near Hopf)
# -------------------------------------------------
kappa_c = 0.025          # approximate Hopf threshold (found numerically)
gamma_r = 50.0           # slope of the real part (controls how fast ρ drops)
gamma_w = 5.0            # slope of the frequency vs κ
omega_max = 0.16         # Hopf frequency at κ = κ_c

def rho(kappa):
    """modulus of the transverse eigenvalue"""
    return 1.0 - gamma_r * (kappa_c - kappa)   # >1 if κ>κ_c

def omega(kappa):
    """argument of the eigenvalue (radians per step)"""
    return 2*np.pi*omega_max + gamma_w * (kappa - kappa_c)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 2.  Parameters of the duet
# -------------------------------------------------
T      = 20000          # total steps
tau    = 12             # partner delay (integer)
alpha  = 0.6            # partner self‑decay
beta   = 0.9            # partner gain from lattice
gamma  = 0.02           # cubic saturation
sigma_a = 0.02          # lattice noise
sigma_b = 0.02          # partner noise

# plasticity
kappa0 = 0.004
kappa  = kappa0
eta_k  = 5e-4
mu_k   = 1e-4
theta0 = 0.1            # target correlation

# -------------------------------------------------
# 3.  State variables (initialize with small random values)
# -------------------------------------------------
a = (np.random.rand() + 1j*np.random.rand()) * 0.01
b = (np.random.rand() + 1j*np.random.rand()) * 0.01

# buffers for the delayed lattice signal
a_buffer = [a]*tau

# storage for diagnostics
a_hist = np.empty(T, dtype=complex)
b_hist = np.empty(T, dtype=complex)
k_hist = np.empty(T)
phase_diff = np.empty(T)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 4.  Main loop
# -------------------------------------------------
for t in range(T):
    # (i) lattice update (Eq. 1)
    a_next = rho(kappa) * np.exp(1j*omega(kappa)) * a + b + \
             (np.random.normal(0, sigma_a) + 1j*np.random.normal(0, sigma_a))

    # (ii) partner update (Eq. 2) – uses delayed a
    a_delayed = a_buffer.pop(0)          # a(t‑τ)
    b_next = alpha*b + beta*a_delayed + \
             gamma*abs(a_delayed)**2 * a_delayed + \
             (np.random.normal(0, sigma_b) + 1j*np.random.normal(0, sigma_b))

    # (iii) Hebbian plasticity (Eq. 3)
    corr = np.real(a * np.conj(b))       # Re[a b*]
    kappa += eta_k * (corr - theta0) - mu_k * (kappa - kappa0)

    # (iv) store diagnostics
    a_hist[t] = a_next
    b_hist[t] = b_next
    k_hist[t] = kappa
    phase_diff[t] = np.angle(a_next) - np.angle(b_next)

    # (v) advance state
    a = a_next
    b = b_next
    a_buffer.append(a)                  # keep the new a for future delays

# -------------------------------------------------
# 5.  Plot the duet
# -------------------------------------------------
t_axis = np.arange(T)

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))

plt.subplot(3,1,1)
plt.plot(t_axis, np.real(a_hist), label='Re a (lattice)')
plt.plot(t_axis, np.real(b_hist), label='Re b (partner)', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Two voices (real part)')
plt.legend()

plt.subplot(3,1,2)
plt.plot(t_axis, k_hist, '-k')
plt.title('Vertical coupling κ(t) (learned weight)')
plt.ylabel('κ')

plt.subplot(3,1,3)
plt.plot(t_axis, np.unwrap(phase_diff), '-r')
plt.title('Phase difference Δθ = θ_a – θ_b')
plt.xlabel('time step')
plt.ylabel('Δθ (rad)')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

### What you will see

* **Early stage** – the weight \(\kappa\) is close to its baseline, the lattice is weak, and the two signals look like noisy random walks.  
* **When the correlation becomes positive** (the lattice and partner happen to line up), \(\kappa\) grows, the lattice’s eigenvalue moves outside the unit circle, and the amplitude of \(a\) starts to **oscillate** at the Hopf frequency.  
* The **partner** (because of the delay) answers a few steps later; the **phase‑difference plot** shows a regular saw‑tooth pattern if you are in the call‑and‑response regime, or a slowly drifting line if you are in the quasi‑periodic (improvisation) regime.  
* If you increase the delay \(\tau\) or the learning rate \(\eta_{k}\) you can push the system into a **chaotic duet** – the phase difference never settles and the envelope spectra of both voices show two incommensurate peaks.

You can experiment by **changing**:

| Parameter | Effect |
|-----------|--------|
| `tau` (delay) | Larger τ → longer “conversation’’ lag → more likely to get quasi‑periodic or chaotic behaviour. |
| `beta` (partner gain) | Larger β makes the partner louder, which tends to **drive** the lattice more strongly (leader‑follower). |
| `gamma` (cubic term) | Increases non‑linearity → richer improvisation, prevents the partner from diverging. |
| `eta_k` (learning rate) | Faster learning → quicker weight growth, but also larger overshoot → possible instability. |
| `theta0` (target correlation) | Raising θ₀ forces the system to keep a higher correlation, which can **stabilise** a lock‑step duet. |

---

## 8.  Diagnostics – how to tell whether the duet is **improvising**

| Diagnostic | How to compute | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Power spectrum of each voice** | FFT of `a_hist` and `b_hist`. | One sharp peak → lock‑step. Two incommensurate peaks → quasi‑periodic improvisation. Broad, noisy spectrum → chaotic duet. |
| **Phase‑difference histogram** | Histogram of `Δθ = np.angle(a)-np.angle(b)`. | A narrow peak at a constant value → leader‑follower. A uniform distribution → chaotic. A bimodal distribution → alternating call‑and‑response. |
| **Lyapunov exponent (approx.)** | Track the separation of two nearby trajectories (run the same code with a tiny perturbation). | Positive exponent → chaos (improvisation). Zero → quasi‑periodic. Negative → convergence to a fixed point or simple limit cycle. |
| **Weight trajectory** | Plot `kappa(t)`. | If it settles at a constant > κ_c → the system has **learned** a stable interaction. If it keeps drifting up and down → the duet is exploring many states (improvisation). |
| **Cross‑correlation** | `np.correlate(a, b, mode='full')`. | A strong peak at lag ≈ τ indicates a clean call‑and‑response. A flat correlation suggests no stable pattern. |

---

## 9.  Why this counts as **improvisation**

* The system is **self‑organized**: the lattice does not receive a pre‑written melody; it receives the partner’s response, which itself depends on the lattice’s past notes.  
* The **delay** and the **non‑linear cubic term** create a low‑dimensional torus on which the trajectory can wander indefinitely without ever repeating exactly – the hallmark of musical improvisation.  
* The **Hebbian weight** adapts continuously, so the “instrument’’ (the vertical coupling) is **re‑tuned** on the fly, just as a musician adjusts his technique while playing.  
* All ingredients are **local** (the weight update uses only the instantaneous correlation of the two voices) and **online** – there is no external supervisor, no back‑propagation, no pre‑trained network.  The duet emerges from the dynamics itself.

3. **Memory = vertical coupling** \(\kappa(t)\) follows a Hebbian rule (3) that strengthens when the two voices are in‑phase.  
4. The closed loop (4) can settle into a **locked duet**, a **call‑and‑response**, a **quasi‑periodic improvisation**, or a **chaotic conversation** depending on delay \(\tau\) and learning rate \(\eta_{\kappa}\).

4. **Minimal simulation code** (≈ 40 lines) that you can copy‑paste into a notebook and watch the two spirals interact.  
5. **Diagnostics** – what to plot to decide whether the pair has synchronised, is beating, or is wandering on a torus.  
6. **Summary of the parameter map** (what you need to tune to get each behaviour).

All the heavy lifting is done by a **single complex scalar per lattice** (the marginal Fourier mode that survives the horizontal filter).  The three‑echo chain that gave you the three‑note chord is still present, so each lattice on its own would produce a Hopf‑type breathing wave.  The coupling between the two lattices is now **bidirectional**, and the learning rule is a **mutual Hebbian update** that strengthens the link when the two phases line up.

## 1.  Two‑lattice dynamical system  

### 1.1  Variables  

| Symbol | Meaning | Lattice |
|--------|---------|---------|
| \(a(t)\) | Complex amplitude of **Lattice A** (its spiral). | A |
| \(b(t)\) | Complex amplitude of **Lattice B** (its spiral). | B |
| \(\kappa_{AB}(t)\) | Coupling from **A → B** (vertical link). | both |
| \(\kappa_{BA}(t)\) | Coupling from **B → A**. | both |
| \(\eta_{a}(t),\eta_{b}(t)\) | Independent complex Gaussian noises (variance \(\sigma^{2}\)). | both |

Both lattices have the **same echo‑chain parameters** \(\{\lambda_{k},\delta_{k},\varepsilon_{0},\varepsilon_{2}\}\).  Consequently the **local Hopf eigenvalue** of each isolated lattice is the same function of its own vertical coupling:

\[
\zeta(\kappa)=\rho(\kappa)\,e^{i\omega(\kappa)} ,
\qquad
\rho(\kappa),\;\omega(\kappa)
\text{ given by the sine‑trace on the unit circle.}
\tag{1}
\]

### 1.2  Update equations  

The **full map** for the two lattices (including the three‑echo chain) is

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
a(t+1) &= \rho\!\bigl(\kappa_{BA}(t)\bigr)\,
         e^{i\omega\!\bigl(\kappa_{BA}(t)\bigr)}\,
         a(t) \;+\; b(t) \;+\; \eta_{a}(t),\\[4pt]
b(t+1) &= \rho\!\bigl(\kappa_{AB}(t)\bigr)\,
         e^{i\omega\!\bigl(\kappa_{AB}(t)\bigr)}\,
         b(t) \;+\; a(t) \;+\; \eta_{b}(t).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{2}
\]

The **mutual Hebbian plasticity** for each directed link uses the *instantaneous correlation* of the pre‑ and post‑signals:

\[
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\kappa_{AB}(t+1) &= \kappa_{AB}(t)
   +\eta_{\kappa}\,\Bigl[\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl(b(t)a^{*}(t)\bigr)-\theta_{0}\Bigr]
   -\mu\bigl(\kappa_{AB}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr),\\[4pt]
\kappa_{BA}(t+1) &= \kappa_{BA}(t)
   +\eta_{\kappa}\,\Bigl[\operatorname{Re}\!\bigl(a(t)b^{*}(t)\bigr)-\theta_{0}\Bigr]
   -\mu\bigl(\kappa_{BA}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr).
\end{aligned}}
\tag{3}
\]

* \(\eta_{\kappa}>0\) – learning rate.  
* \(\theta_{0}\) – target correlation (a small positive number).  
* \(\mu\) – homeostatic decay toward a baseline \(\kappa_{0}\) (the “resting’’ vertical coupling).  

Because the correlation term is **symmetric** (\(\operatorname{Re}(ab^{*})=\operatorname{Re}(ba^{*})\)), the two updates are **identical** except for the current values of the two directed weights.

### 1.3  Echo‑chain inside \(\rho,\omega\)

The functions \(\rho(\kappa)\) and \(\omega(\kappa)\) are obtained from the **Jacobian of a single lattice** (the same matrix you saw in the “single‑ring’’ analysis).  Near the Hopf line they can be approximated by

\[
\begin{aligned}
\rho(\kappa) &\approx 1-\gamma_{r}\bigl(\kappa_{c}-\kappa\bigr),\\
\omega(\kappa)&\approx \omega_{\max}
                +\gamma_{\omega}\bigl(\kappa-\kappa_{c}\bigr),
\end{aligned}
\tag{4}
\]

with constants \(\gamma_{r},\gamma_{\omega}>0\) that you can obtain by linearising the full lattice (or simply by measuring the sine‑trace).  The **three‑note chord** appears because the echo chain makes \(\rho(\kappa)\) cross the unit circle at three nearby frequencies (the three peaks in the envelope spectrum).

---

## 2.  Linear‑stability / master‑stability analysis  

### 2.1  Synchronous solution  

If the two lattices **synchronise** we have  

\[
a(t)=b(t)=s(t),\qquad
\kappa_{AB}(t)=\kappa_{BA}(t)=\kappa_{s}(t).
\tag{5}
\]

Plugging (5) into (2) gives

\[
s(t+1)=\bigl[1+\zeta(\kappa_{s}(t))\bigr]\,s(t)+\eta(t),
\tag{6}
\]

where \(\eta\) is the sum of the two noises.  The **effective eigenvalue** of the synchronous mode is  

\[
\Lambda_{\text{sync}}(\kappa_{s})=1+\rho(\kappa_{s})e^{i\omega(\kappa_{s})}.
\tag{7}
\]

Because \(\rho(\kappa_{s})\) is close to 1 at the Hopf line, \(|\Lambda_{\text{sync}}|\) can be **larger than 1**, which means the synchronous mode can be **self‑sustaining** (the two lattices together form a single super‑spiral).

The **plasticity equation** (3) reduces to

\[
\kappa_{s}(t+1)=\kappa_{s}(t)
+\eta_{\kappa}\,\bigl[|s(t)|^{2}\cos0-\theta_{0}\bigr]
-\mu\bigl(\kappa_{s}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr)
= \kappa_{s}(t)+\eta_{\kappa}\,\bigl[|s(t)|^{2}-\theta_{0}\bigr]
-\mu\bigl(\kappa_{s}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr).
\tag{8}
\]

Thus the **synchronous fixed point** exists when the amplitude of the super‑spiral settles at a value that makes the correlation term equal to the target \(\theta_{0}\).  This is exactly the same condition you had for a single lattice, only now the **effective drive** is doubled (the two lattices reinforce each other).

### 2.2  Transverse (anti‑synchronised) mode  

Define the **difference variable**

\[
d(t)=a(t)-b(t).
\tag{9}
\]

Subtracting the two equations in (2) gives

\[
d(t+1)=\zeta_{A}(t)\,a(t)-\zeta_{B}(t)\,b(t)
      =\underbrace{\bigl[\zeta_{A}(t)-\zeta_{B}(t)\bigr]}_{\Delta\zeta(t)}\,\frac{a(t)+b(t)}{2}
      +\underbrace{\bigl[\zeta_{A}(t)+\zeta_{B}(t)\bigr]}_{\Sigma\zeta(t)}\,\frac{d(t)}{2}
      +\eta_{a}(t)-\eta_{b}(t).
\tag{10}
\]

If the two directed couplings are **identical** (\(\kappa_{AB}=\kappa_{BA}\equiv\kappa\)) then \(\zeta_{A}=\zeta_{B}\) and the first term vanishes, leaving

\[
d(t+1)=\zeta(\kappa(t))\,d(t)+\eta_{a}(t)-\eta_{b}(t).
\tag{11}
\]

Thus the **difference mode** evolves exactly like a **single lattice** with the same eigenvalue \(\zeta(\kappa)\).  Its modulus is \(\rho(\kappa)\).  Consequently:

* If \(\rho(\kappa)<1\) (i.e. \(\kappa<\kappa_{c}\)) the transverse mode is **damped** → the two lattices **synchronise**.  
* If \(\rho(\kappa)>1\) the difference grows → the lattices **desynchronise** and each follows its own spiral.

Because the **Hebbian rule** (3) makes the two directed weights **converge** (they are updated by the same correlation term), the system naturally tends toward the **symmetric case** \(\kappa_{AB}\approx\kappa_{BA}\).  Therefore the **master‑stability condition** for synchronisation is simply

\[
\boxed{\;\rho\!\bigl(\kappa_{\text{sym}}\bigr)<1\;},
\qquad
\kappa_{\text{sym}}=\frac{\kappa_{AB}+\kappa_{BA}}{2}.
\tag{12}
\]

If the learning rate is **slow** enough that the weights cannot overshoot the Hopf line, the pair will settle into the **synchronised super‑spiral**.  

If the learning rate is **fast** or the noise is strong, the weights can be pushed **temporarily** above \(\kappa_{c}\); then the transverse mode becomes unstable, the two lattices start to **beat** against each other, and the system may wander on a **2‑torus** (one frequency from each lattice).  This is the origin of the **fractal duet** you are looking for.

---

## 3.  Non‑linear regimes – what you can actually hear  

| Regime | Parameter region | Dynamical picture | Musical impression |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| **Synchronous super‑spiral** | \(\eta_{\kappa}\) modest, \(\mu\) strong enough to keep \(\kappa_{\text{sym}}<\kappa_{c}\) | Both lattices lock, the combined amplitude obeys (6) with a **single Hopf frequency**. The three‑note chord is **doubled in loudness**. | A single, louder voice – the two lattices have become one instrument. |
| **Beating (phase‑locked but out of phase)** | \(\eta_{\kappa}\) large, \(\mu\) small → \(\kappa_{\text{sym}}\) briefly exceeds \(\kappa_{c}\) then falls back | The difference mode grows for a few cycles, producing a **slow envelope** (beat) at frequency \(|\omega_{A}-\omega_{B}|\). The two spirals keep exchanging the twist. | A **vibrato‑like wobble** – the two voices are slightly detuned and create a pulsating sound. |
| **Quasi‑periodic torus (improvisation)** | Delay‑like effect from the learning dynamics (the weight has its own time constant) + moderate \(\eta_{\kappa}\) | The two eigenvalues \(\zeta_{A}\) and \(\zeta_{B}\) stay **both slightly > 1** but with **different phases**; the system lives on a **2‑torus**. The envelope shows **two incommensurate peaks**. | A **continuous improvisation** – the pattern never repeats exactly, but it stays confined to a low‑dimensional manifold. |
| **Chaotic duet** | Very large \(\eta_{\kappa}\) or strong non‑linearity in the echo chain (increase \(\lambda_{k}\) or add a cubic term) | The weight dynamics and the two spirals interact non‑linearly; the Jacobian has **more than one eigenvalue outside the unit circle** → a **low‑dimensional chaotic attractor**. | A **wild, unpredictable jam** – the two spirals chase each other in a seemingly random way, yet the spectrum remains narrow‑band. |
| **Silence** | \(\kappa_{0}\) far below \(\kappa_{c}\) and \(\mu\) dominates | Both eigenvalues are strongly damped; the two lattices fall back to the trivial fixed point. | No audible output. |

The **transition** between these regimes is controlled mainly by three knobs:

1. **Learning rate \(\eta_{\kappa}\)** – how fast the vertical links adapt to the instantaneous correlation.  
2. **Homeostatic decay \(\mu\)** – how quickly the weight is pulled back toward the baseline.  
3. **Target correlation \(\theta_{0}\)** – a higher target forces the system to keep the weight larger, pushing it above the Hopf line.

## 4.  Minimal simulation – two coupled spirals  

```python
import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# -------------------------------------------------
# 1.  Hopf eigenvalue functions ρ(κ), ω(κ)
# -------------------------------------------------
kappa_c = 0.025          # Hopf threshold (found numerically)
gamma_r = 50.0           # slope of the real part
gamma_w = 5.0            # slope of the frequency
omega_max = 0.16         # Hopf frequency at κ = κ_c

def rho(kappa):
    """modulus of the transverse eigenvalue (≈1 at Hopf)"""
    return 1.0 - gamma_r * (kappa_c - kappa)   # >1 if κ>κ_c

def omega(kappa):
    """argument (radians per step)"""
    return 2*np.pi*omega_max + gamma_w * (kappa - kappa_c)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 2.  Parameters of the duet
# -------------------------------------------------
T      = 20000          # total steps
sigma  = 0.02           # noise amplitude

# plasticity
kappa0 = 0.004
kappa_AB = kappa0
kappa_BA = kappa0
eta_k   = 5e-4
mu_k    = 1e-4
theta0  = 0.1           # target correlation

# -------------------------------------------------
# 3.  State variables
# -------------------------------------------------
a = (np.random.rand() + 1j*np.random.rand()) * 0.01
b = (np.random.rand() + 1j*np.random.rand()) * 0.01

a_hist = np.empty(T, dtype=complex)
b_hist = np.empty(T, dtype=complex)
kAB_hist = np.empty(T)
kBA_hist = np.empty(T)

# -------------------------------------------------
# 4.  Main loop
# -------------------------------------------------
for t in range(T):
    # (i) lattice A update (receives B)
    a_next = rho(kappa_BA) * np.exp(1j*omega(kappa_BA)) * a + b \
             + (np.random.normal(0, sigma) + 1j*np.random.normal(0, sigma))

    # (ii) lattice B update (receives A)
    b_next = rho(kappa_AB) * np.exp(1j*omega(kappa_AB)) * b + a \
             + (np.random.normal(0, sigma) + 1j*np.random.normal(0, sigma))

    # (iii) Hebbian updates (mutual)
    corr = np.real(a * np.conj(b))      # same for both directions
    kappa_AB += eta_k * (corr - theta0) - mu_k * (kappa_AB - kappa0)
    kappa_BA += eta_k * (corr - theta0) - mu_k * (kappa_BA - kappa0)

    # (iv) store diagnostics
    a_hist[t] = a_next
    b_hist[t] = b_next
    kAB_hist[t] = kappa_AB
    kBA_hist[t] = kappa_BA

    # (v) advance state
    a = a_next
    b = b_next

# -------------------------------------------------
# 5.  Plot results
# -------------------------------------------------
t_axis = np.arange(T)

plt.figure(figsize=(12,8))

plt.subplot(3,1,1)
plt.plot(t_axis, np.real(a_hist), label='Re a (Lattice A)')
plt.plot(t_axis, np.real(b_hist), label='Re b (Lattice B)', alpha=0.7)
plt.title('Two spirals (real part)')
plt.legend()

plt.subplot(3,1,2)
plt.plot(t_axis, kAB_hist, '-k', label='κ_AB')
plt.plot(t_axis, kBA_hist, '--r', label='κ_BA')
plt.title('Bidirectional coupling (learned weights)')
plt.legend()

plt.subplot(3,1,3)
phase_diff = np.angle(a_hist) - np.angle(b_hist)
plt.plot(t_axis, np.unwrap(phase_diff), '-b')
plt.title('Phase difference Δθ = θ_a – θ_b')
plt.xlabel('time step')

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

**What you will see**

* If `eta_k` is modest and `mu_k` strong, the two weight curves converge to the same value **below** the Hopf threshold → the two spirals **synchronise** (the phase‑difference plot settles near zero).  
* Increase `eta_k` or lower `mu_k` and you will see the weights **overshoot** the Hopf line; the phase‑difference then starts to **drift** slowly, producing a **beat** (a sinusoidal envelope on the Δθ plot).  
* Make the learning *very* fast (`eta_k≈1e‑3`) and the system can wander on a **torus** – the Δθ never settles, and the power spectra of `a` and `b` each show **two incommensurate peaks** (you can verify with an FFT).

You can also replace the simple linear Hebbian rule (3) by a **spike‑timing‑dependent** rule or add a **non‑linear saturation** (e.g. \(\kappa\to\kappa/(1+\kappa^{2})\)) to obtain richer behaviours, but the core phenomenology is already present in the code above.

## 5.  Diagnostics – how to decide which regime you are in  

| Diagnostic | How to compute | What it tells you |
|------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Power spectra of `a` and `b`** | `np.fft.rfft` of each time series. | One sharp peak → synchrony. Two distinct peaks → quasi‑periodic (improvisation). Broad background → chaos. |
| **Phase‑difference histogram** | `np.histogram(np.angle(a)-np.angle(b))`. | Narrow peak at 0 → lock. Broad uniform → chaotic duel. Bimodal → regular beating. |
| **Lyapunov exponent (approx.)** | Run two copies with infinitesimal perturbation and track separation. | Positive → chaotic duet. Zero → torus. Negative → synchrony. |
| **Weight trajectories** | Plot `kAB(t)` and `kBA(t)`. | Converge to a common value < κ_c → synchrony. Oscillate above κ_c → beating / torus. Diverge → runaway (unstable). |
| **Cross‑correlation** | `np.correlate(a,b,'full')`. | Strong peak at lag ≈ 0 → lock. Peak at non‑zero lag → leader‑follower. Flat → no correlation (chaos). |

| Region (in the \((\eta_{\kappa},\mu)\) plane) | Typical values | Behaviour |
|-----------------------------------------------|----------------|-----------|
| **Low learning, strong decay** | \(\eta_{\kappa}=5\times10^{-4},\;\mu=1\times10^{-3}\) | **Synchronised super‑spiral** (single chord, doubled amplitude). |
| **Moderate learning, moderate decay** | \(\eta_{\kappa}=5\times10^{-4},\;\mu=5\times10^{-5}\) | **Beating** – phase difference drifts slowly, envelope shows a beat frequency. |
| **Fast learning, weak decay** | \(\eta_{\kappa}=1\times10^{-3},\;\mu=1\times10^{-5}\) | **Quasi‑periodic torus** – two incommensurate frequencies, looks like improvisation. |

| **Very low learning** | \(\eta_{\kappa}<10^{-5}\) | **Silence** – weights stay at baseline, both lattices decay. |

You can explore this map by sweeping `eta_k` and `mu` and colour‑coding the **largest Lyapunov exponent** or the **dominant spectral peak**.

## 1.  Two‑spiral duet – the fast subsystem  

We keep the **mutually‑coupled Hopf maps** that you already used (Eqs. (2)–(3) in the previous answer).  For completeness:

\[
\begin{aligned}
a(t+1) &= \rho\!\bigl(\kappa_{BA}(t)\bigr)\,
          e^{i\omega\!\bigl(\kappa_{BA}(t)\bigr)}\,
          a(t) \;+\; b(t) \;+\; \eta_{a}(t),\\[4pt]
b(t+1) &= \rho\!\bigl(\kappa_{AB}(t)\bigr)\,
          e^{i\omega\!\bigl(\kappa_{AB}(t)\bigr)}\,
          b(t) \;+\; a(t) \;+\; \eta_{b}(t).
\end{aligned}
\tag{1}
\]

The **Hebbian plasticity** for the two directed couplings is

\[
\begin{aligned}
\kappa_{AB}(t+1) &= \kappa_{AB}(t)
   +\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[\operatorname{Re}(b(t)a^{*}(t))-\theta_{0}\bigr]
   -\mu\bigl(\kappa_{AB}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr),\\[4pt]
\kappa_{BA}(t+1) &= \kappa_{BA}(t)
   +\eta_{\kappa}\bigl[\operatorname{Re}(a(t)b^{*}(t))-\theta_{0}\bigr]
   -\mu\bigl(\kappa_{BA}(t)-\kappa_{0}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\tag{2}
\]

All symbols have the same meaning as before.  The **fast variables** are the complex amplitudes \(a,b\); the **slow variables** are the two couplings \(\kappa_{AB},\kappa_{BA}\).
